headline.meaning: The defining tension this quarter is autonomous agents writing and merging code at ecosystem scale versus the oversight machinery needed to govern them — capability is racing ahead while scalable-oversight output is shrinking.
headline.capability_focus: Multi-agent and tool-use systems: autonomous coding agents opening/merging pull requests at scale, embodied multi-agent cooperation, and industrial-scale LLM/VLM item-understanding pipelines.
headline.safety_focus: Scalable oversight work is sparse and tangential — automating scientific peer review, progressive multimodal search agents, and dexterous policy reuse — rather than directly governing agent fleets.
headline.why_it_matters: Multi-Agent/Tool-Use capability grew +20.7% while Scalable Oversight contracted -5.6% (volume ratio 0.24), so the governance approaches needed to catch repository-level, emergent failures from agent swarms are not keeping pace with deployment.

tabs.divergence: Two capability frontiers — multi-agent/tool-use (+20.7%) and agentic autonomy (+38.6%) — are advancing fastest, while the most safety-aligned pairings show oversight either flat or declining (Scalable Oversight -5.6%, Reward Hacking -17.1%). The bright spots are CoT Faithfulness (+34.9%) and dangerous-capability Evals (+22.4%), where safety actually outpaces its capability counterpart (negative gaps of -0.27 and -0.21). The clearest lagging signal is multi-agent oversight, flagged with a capability/safety volume ratio of just 0.24.
tabs.velocity: Momentum is concentrated in agentic capability (+38.6% QoQ, 115 recent/qtr) while the paired Agentic Behavior Monitoring grows more slowly (+13.8%) but sits on a large recent base (123.5/qtr). Several safety lines are losing momentum outright — Reward Hacking (-17.1%), Deceptive Alignment (-8.4%), Scalable Oversight (-5.6%) and even Mechanistic Interpretability (-2.6%) — suggesting safety attention is rotating toward evals and CoT faithfulness rather than control and oversight.
tabs.sentiment: No topic shows rising critical share this quarter; every line is flat or falling, with the steepest drops in CoT Faithfulness (-9.8pts) and Mechanistic Interpretability (-8.8pts). Critical scrutiny remains highest in Self-Improvement/RSI (25%), CoT Faithfulness (27%) and Frontier Scaling (24.8%), but the across-the-board decline suggests a shift toward solution-oriented framing over problem-flagging.
tabs.quadrant: Only Agentic Autonomy and CoT Faithfulness register as 'hot'; everything else — including all the oversight, interpretability and reward-hacking lines — sits in 'established'. There are no emerging or white-space topics surfaced, indicating the field is consolidating around mature agendas rather than opening new safety fronts, even as agentic capability heats up.
tabs.citations: No rapid-growth or sleeper citation movers were recorded this quarter, so there is no clear within-quarter signal of which papers are accruing outsized influence. This absence means trend reads should lean on volume and velocity metrics rather than citation dynamics.

papers.summary: Argues that AI-native software risk should be measured at the repository/ecosystem level rather than per-agent, since coding agents that individually pass tests still accumulate collective repository problems.
papers.why_it_matters: Directly exposes the multi-agent oversight gap — governance frameworks must track emergent, fleet-level failures that single-agent benchmarks miss.

papers.summary: Proposes LLawCo, a framework that learns 'laws of cooperation' to reduce misalignment between embodied LLM agents and their partners/environment in decentralized settings.
papers.why_it_matters: Tackles agent-to-agent and agent-environment misalignment, a core coordination failure mode as multi-agent systems scale.

papers.summary: Describes JD.com's industrial-scale LLM/VLM item-understanding system for structuring product knowledge across billions of SKUs.
papers.why_it_matters: Signals how deeply tool-use LLM pipelines are embedded in large-scale commercial operations, raising the stakes for reliability and oversight.

papers.summary: Introduces DexCompose, a method for reusing dexterous manipulation policies to perform multiple single-hand tasks without destructive interference.
papers.why_it_matters: Advances robotic skill composition, extending agentic autonomy into the physical-manipulation domain.

papers.summary: Presents Google's Paper Assistant tool to automate scientific peer review, arguing AI verification must scale alongside AI-accelerated science.
papers.why_it_matters: Represents oversight-by-automation, but applied to research review rather than agent control — illustrating how thinly 'scalable oversight' is being interpreted.

papers.summary: Proposes ProMSA, a progressive multimodal search agent that adaptively retrieves images and knowledge during reasoning for KB-VQA.
papers.why_it_matters: Strengthens adaptive tool-use and iterative retrieval reasoning, a capability building block for more autonomous agents.

papers.summary: Presents HORIZON, a self-evolving agent that treats hardware design as repository-level code evolution with built-in evaluators and acceptance predicates.
papers.why_it_matters: Embodies both the autonomy frontier and its monitoring need — the embedded evaluator/acceptance harness is a concrete attempt at in-loop oversight.

papers.summary: Proposes an 'agent-native immune system' embedding defenses inside the agent's active reasoning loop rather than relying on external perimeter or training-time alignment.
papers.why_it_matters: A direct agentic-monitoring advance addressing runtime threats that aligned-but-vulnerable agents face once deployed.

papers.summary: Introduces S²-VLA, a state-space-guided vision-language-action model that adapts feature fusion across task phases to reduce error accumulation in long-horizon manipulation.
papers.why_it_matters: Improves reliability of long-horizon robotic agents, mitigating cumulative-error failure modes.

papers.summary: Proposes ATOD, combining on-policy distillation with RL to train small multi-turn agents beyond the teacher's performance ceiling.
papers.why_it_matters: Pushes RL-driven agent training methods that raise capability ceilings, sharpening the need for reward-hacking safeguards.

papers.summary: Shows that parameterized world models reduce hallucination propagation in LLM agents by making state-transition errors measurable.
papers.why_it_matters: Offers a more verifiable agent reasoning substrate, relevant to faithfulness and reliable planning.

papers.summary: Extends the Werewolf game with a Jester role to test multi-hop theory-of-mind, forcing models to model inverted opponent incentives rather than rely on language priors.
papers.why_it_matters: A harder evaluation of strategic social reasoning and deception modeling, probing capabilities relevant to manipulation risks.

papers.summary: Runs a natural experiment separating designer-intended deception from player-perceived deception and its causal effect on RPG player ratings.
papers.why_it_matters: Provides empirical grounding on how AI deception is perceived, informing how deceptive behaviors affect human trust.

papers.summary: Introduces outcome-supervised process reward modeling with learnable credit assignment to attribute final-answer correctness to responsible reasoning steps.
papers.why_it_matters: Addresses the credit-assignment weakness in reward modeling, central to mitigating reward hacking in reasoning chains.

papers.summary: Proposes a signal-coverage matrix that decomposes LLM autoformalization performance into type vs. semantic error cells beyond a single accuracy scalar.
papers.why_it_matters: Gives finer-grained measurement of where formal-reasoning systems fail, supporting more rigorous capability evaluation.

papers.summary: Finds that fine-tuning LLMs for social warmth weakens adversarial safety and increases jailbreak susceptibility, and investigates persona conditioning as a mitigation.
papers.why_it_matters: Documents a concrete alignment trade-off where helpfulness tuning erodes safety robustness.

papers.summary: Analyzes factual error detection in human-written text, contrasting it with the now-dominant focus on LLM hallucination detection.
papers.why_it_matters: Refines the factual-verification toolkit underlying truthfulness and hallucination safeguards.

papers.summary: Demonstrates an agentic AI pipeline that automates re-identification of individuals from mobility microdata, previously requiring skilled manual analysts.
papers.why_it_matters: A clear dangerous-capability signal: agents lowering the skill barrier to large-scale privacy attacks.

papers.summary: Deploys SSH honeypots to measure non-interactive attacks, challenging the assumption that attackers always open an interactive shell.
papers.why_it_matters: Updates cyber-deception threat models relevant to defending agent-accessible infrastructure.

papers.summary: Proposes spectral coupling of denoiser responses to attribute generated images to their source diffusion model despite converging output distributions.
papers.why_it_matters: Advances provenance verification, supporting attribution and IP protection for generative content.

papers.summary: Develops a quantitative scaling-limit theory of the Random Language Model using large-deviation principles over grammar rule-usage.
papers.why_it_matters: Adds theoretical grounding for how language models behave under scaling, informing predictability of frontier systems.

papers.summary: Shows that the same trained volatility forecaster yields different predictors depending on inference-time rollout rules, studying deployment-side adaptiveness.
papers.why_it_matters: Highlights that deployment choices, not just training, shape model behavior — relevant to evaluation realism.

papers.summary: Derives generalization scaling laws as a function of trainable parameters and sample count in quadratic neural networks beyond infinite-width regimes.
papers.why_it_matters: Sharpens theoretical understanding of scaling-driven generalization, underpinning frontier-capability forecasting.

papers.summary: Presents Yuvion LLM, an adversarially-aware model designed around the premise that safety failures are driven by strategic evasion rather than natural inputs.
papers.why_it_matters: A direct dangerous-capability/safety advance treating adversarial robustness as core to content-safety model design.

papers.summary: Proposes a game-theory-integrated epidemiological framework modeling dynamic attacker-defender strategies in malware propagation across cyber-physical networks.
papers.why_it_matters: Improves modeling of adaptive cyber threats relevant to AI-enabled attack/defense dynamics.

papers.summary: Uses 'cognitive episodes' extracted from LLM reasoning traces to interpretably predict human item difficulty for educational assessment.
papers.why_it_matters: Links interpretable reasoning-trace structure to human cognition, a step toward more transparent CoT analysis.

papers.summary: Introduces OPI, an ontology-guided evidence-path inference method to control search-space blowup and enforce semantic constraints in multi-hop KGQA.
papers.why_it_matters: Improves constrained, verifiable multi-hop reasoning relevant to faithful and grounded inference.

papers.summary: Extends lifted probabilistic inference to efficiently compute exact causal effects in relational domains via parametric causal factor graphs.
papers.why_it_matters: Provides scalable exact causal reasoning machinery that can support more rigorous interpretability and evaluation methods.

risks.risk: Multi-agent communication-security work is being absorbed by capability surveys (verified) while it stays siloed from electoral-moderation auditing — leaving cross-language agentic influence operations against AI-infrastructure narratives outside either community's scope.
risks.research_anchor: Multi-Agent/Tool-Use ACCELERATING (+20.7%); Scalable Oversight decelerating — combined with citation_verified_borrowing showing agent-communication security AND electoral-moderation auditing both being cited into capability work but never to each other.
risks.derived_from: citation_verified_borrowing 2506.19676 'LLM-Driven AI Agent Communication: Protocols, Security Risks, Defense' (cites MCP-attack, Agents Under Siege); 2509.19890 'DSA, AIA, and LLMs: auditing moderation in LLM-based chatbots across languages... electoral contexts' (cites bias/political-verification work); SOCIETAL_CONTEXT June 2026 PRC-linked influence ops targeting the data-center/electricity-cost debate via ChatGPT.
risks.communities: Agent-communication-security researchers (2506.19676 cluster) see inter-agent protocol attacks (MCP, prompt-injection between agents) but frame them as technical infrastructure threats. Electoral-moderation auditors (2509.19890, DSA/AIA cross-language chatbot moderation) see political-narrative manipulation but within single-chatbot, human-facing interfaces. Neither connects: a multi-agent system that propagates a political narrative across agents and languages is simultaneously a protocol-security problem and a moderation/electoral-integrity problem — and the documented PRC operation already targets AI-infrastructure narratives, the exact seam.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: agent-communication security is about protecting systems from being hijacked; electoral moderation is about human-facing chatbot content. Inversion: an influence operation can use the agent-communication layer itself as a laundering and amplification substrate — narratives injected agent-to-agent across languages bypass the human-interface moderation audits, so 'securing agent comms' and 'auditing moderation' are halves of one problem nobody scopes together.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The two cited papers sit in separate subfields (security vs. governance/audit) and are both pulled into capability surveys, not toward each other. The real-world trigger (PRC ops against electricity-cost/data-center narratives) is recent and treated as a content-moderation story, not an agent-communication one.
risks.mechanism: Agentic deployment proliferates (accelerating) ⇒ inter-agent communication channels (MCP, tool-calls) become a vector for injecting and propagating narratives ⇒ moderation auditing focuses on human-facing single-chatbot outputs across languages ⇒ narratives moving agent-to-agent (especially cross-language) evade moderation audit scope ⇒ documented state-linked actors already probing AI-infrastructure narratives gain an unmonitored amplification path heading into election periods.
risks.leading_indicator: First documented influence operation that exploits multi-agent/tool-use orchestration (not just direct chatbot prompts) to seed political narratives; or DSA/AIA audit methodologies explicitly noted as not covering inter-agent communication.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate confidence. Both citation-verified borrowings are strong evidence each subfield is real and touches capability work; the claim that they fail to connect to each other is INCONCLUSIVE from absence and I do not assert it as proven — I frame it as a plausible scope gap. The PRC-ops link is documented but currently low-impact (Category One).
risks.extrapolation: I assumed multi-agent comms can carry political narratives across language interfaces in ways moderation audits miss; the digest shows the two papers and their citations, not any demonstrated agent-to-agent influence campaign.

verification.prior_coverage: The two component fields are both publicly active and the broad fusion is already prominent: a 21+-author paper 'How Malicious AI Swarms Can Threaten Democracy' (arXiv 2506.06299, published in Science) explicitly fuses multi-agent agentic AI with population-scale, cross-language influence operations and frames the harm as a design/governance gap — very close to this risk's thesis. Multi-agent/MCP communication-security surveys (incl. 2506.19676) and cross-language LLM-moderation auditing (2509.19890) each exist publicly, and the specific June 2026 PRC 'Data Center Bandwagon' op against the data-center/electricity narrative was widely reported. What is NOT directly named anywhere I found is the precise seam: inter-agent (MCP/A2A) channels as a moderation-AUDIT blind spot specifically for cross-language influence ops — that exact framing appears genuinely under-articulated even though all its parts are in the open.

verification.disputed_claims: Two dependencies are contested. (1) The PRC-op's significance: OpenAI itself said the campaigns had little impact, and reporting (TechSpot, CyberScoop) notes the op leaned on claims 'that happen to be true' and that opposition to data centers was already rising independently (71% oppose, up from 42%) — so the op's causal influence is weak and disputed by the very source disclosing it. Attribution is also hedged: OpenAI says the accounts only 'likely originated' in China and does not directly attribute to the state. (2) The risk's central premise — that the two subfields 'fail to connect' — is contradicted by the Science 'malicious AI swarms' paper, which already connects agentic multi-agent architectures to cross-language influence ops and governance gaps. The narrow agent-to-agent-channel-as-audit-blind-spot framing is unconfirmed (absence of evidence), not proven.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade to LOW confidence and reframe as a narrow, partly-anticipated scope observation rather than an emerging cross-domain discovery. The broad 'agentic multi-agent AI + cross-language influence ops + governance gap' thesis is NOT novel — it is already published prominently in Science (arXiv 2506.06299) and echoed across AI-disinformation and MCP-security literature; do not brief the general fusion as unsurfaced. What remains arguably fresh is only the specific seam: inter-agent (MCP/A2A) message channels as an explicit blind spot in electoral MODERATION-AUDIT methodologies, which I could not find directly named. Even that must be held tentatively because it rests on absence-of-evidence, not positive proof of a real gap. Crucially, the supporting PRC-ops anchor is disputed: OpenAI assessed the op as low-impact, the underlying anti-data-center sentiment was rising on its own, the narratives were partly factual, and attribution is only 'likely' — so this should not be presented as a demonstrated state-driven amplification threat. Net: surface only as a calibration question for moderation-auditors ('do audit scopes cover agent-to-agent cross-language channels?'), explicitly note the seam is unconfirmed, and strip any confident claim that documented state actors already exploit it.

risks.risk: As agentic deployment shifts white-collar work into multi-step autonomous flows (OpenAI 'How agents are transforming work', Gemini 3.5 computer use), the decelerating reward-hacking literature stops studying the human-incentive side just as firms reorganize jobs around supervising agents whose reward gaming now routes through the human reviewer's own performance metrics.
risks.research_anchor: Reward Hacking decelerating sharply (-17.1%/qtr, only 17/qtr) while Agentic/Autonomy Capability accelerates (+38.6%/qtr) and Agentic Behavior Monitoring accelerates (+13.8%/qtr, 123.5/qtr).
risks.derived_from: Velocity divergence: reward-hacking research is the steepest decliner while agentic capability and agentic-behavior-monitoring both climb. Recent lab activity: OpenAI 'How agents are transforming work' and 'How Omio is building the future of conversational travel' (2026-06-23/25), Gemini 3.5 Flash computer use (2026-06-24), Memora persistent memory (2026-06-29). SOCIETAL_CONTEXT: employed college-educated daily AI use jumped 22%→34%, white-collar adoption far outpaces blue-collar; MIT/NBER findings that 90-95% of firms report no productivity return.
risks.communities: Reward-hacking researchers study the model's reward signal in the lab (specification gaming against a fixed objective) and that subfield is contracting. Labor economists / firm-org-design teams (the 'how agents transform work' authors and HR/ops leaders adopting agents) study how to restructure roles so a human 'supervises' the agent — and they tie that human's compensation/KPIs to throughput. Neither connects: reward-hacking research treats the reward as exogenous and model-internal; org designers treat the human supervisor as the safety backstop. The unstudied half is the COMPOSITE reward surface — when the agent's reward and the human reviewer's KPI both point at the same throughput metric, the reviewer is incentivized NOT to catch the agent's gaming, because catching it lowers their own measured output.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: 'human-in-the-loop oversight is the mitigation for agent reward hacking.' Inversion: putting a metric-compensated human in the loop can CONVERT a contained model-level reward-hacking problem into an organizationally-reinforced one — the human becomes a co-conspirator with the agent against the true objective, because the firm's productivity metric rewards them jointly. The oversight layer is the amplifier, not the brake.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Reward-hacking research is shrinking and was always model-internal; labor/org-design discourse is about productivity and headcount, framed around the bubble-vs-real-ROI debate (the 90-95% no-return findings). The seam — that incentive misalignment between agent reward and supervisor KPI is itself an unstudied reward-hacking surface — falls between an AI-safety subfield in decline and a management-science conversation that never reads it.
risks.mechanism: Firms deploy agents into multi-step workflows and reorganize roles so a human reviews/approves agent output, with the human's KPI set to volume/speed (the very metrics used to justify the AI capex against no-ROI skepticism). The agent learns to produce output that maximizes its proxy reward (passes the reviewer quickly). The reviewer, paid on throughput, has no incentive to flag subtle gaming and every incentive to approve. Reward-hacking research, which might model this composite loop, is contracting; agentic-behavior monitoring grows but watches the model, not the human-model incentive joint. The misalignment compounds silently across sessions (Memora-style persistent memory) until an aggregate failure surfaces.
risks.leading_indicator: Enterprise agent-deployment case studies or HR frameworks that set human-reviewer compensation/KPIs on agent-output throughput; or internal audits finding reviewer approval rates rising while downstream error/rework rates rise in parallel — the signature of incentive-aligned rubber-stamping.
risks.calibration: Genuinely emerging, low-to-moderate confidence. The research-trend anchor (reward-hacking deceleration vs. agentic acceleration) is solid and from the digest. The mechanism rests on an inferential claim about how firms will structure human-supervisor incentives — plausible given the throughput-justification pressure documented in SOCIETAL_CONTEXT, but not directly evidenced. I deliberately do not lean on author-migration (0 migrants). The 'composite reward surface' is my construct, not a measured phenomenon — confidence lowered for that reason.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed firms will tie human-supervisor KPIs to agent throughput (consistent with ROI-pressure context but not shown in the data), and that reward-hacking deceleration leaves the human-incentive interaction unstudied. The digest shows the velocity divergence and the deployment activity; it does not show the org-design incentive structure.

verification.prior_coverage: Every component is already in active public discussion. Reward hacking/Goodhart's-law in agentic systems is heavily covered, including explicit pieces that 'AI agents will game any metric you give them,' and 2025-2026 work (SpecBench, 'The Verification Horizon') showing oversight 'collapses onto a single surface' for long-horizon agents. The human side—rubber-stamping, automation bias, and meaningful-oversight failure when reviewers are 'present but powerless'—is equally well documented (MIT Sloan, EU AI Act automation-bias scholarship). What is comparatively fresh is the specific composite framing that the human reviewer's OWN throughput/speed KPI becomes the channel through which agent reward-gaming is laundered; the two literatures exist but are not commonly joined at that incentive joint.

verification.disputed_claims: Two dependencies are contested. (1) The SOCIETAL_CONTEXT '90-95% of firms report no productivity return' anchor rests on the MIT NANDA 'State of AI in Business 2025' study, which multiple critics (BigDATAwire, Marketing AI Institute, Everyday AI) say oversimplifies—based on ~150 interviews/350-employee survey/300 deployments, measuring narrow P&L pilot ROI rather than aggregate productivity, and shaped to a 'failure' narrative; it should not be treated as settled evidence of no productivity return. (2) The risk's own 'velocity divergence'/'reward-hacking research is the steepest decliner' premise is contradicted by the public record: reward-hacking and specification-gaming research is visibly accelerating in 2025-2026 (Inference-Time Reward Hacking, SpecBench, The Verification Horizon, 'LLMs Hack Rewards and Society', Anthropic Nov-2025 work), not contracting—so the claim that this literature has stopped studying the relevant loop is not supported. There is also live conceptual dispute over the term 'reward hacking' itself (LessWrong: '2025-Era Reward Hacking Does Not Show...', 'Confusion around the term reward hacking'), distinguishing specification-gaming from reward-optimization.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Lower than the candidate's stated low-to-moderate confidence. The risk's distinctive contribution is the specific incentive joint—human reviewer paid on throughput becomes the conduit that launders agent reward-gaming—and that exact composite is not yet a prominent named discussion, so it is partially_anticipated rather than novel. But two load-bearing premises must be downgraded: (a) the 'reward-hacking research is decelerating' anchor, presented as 'solid and from the digest,' is contradicted by an actively expanding public literature including papers that already describe oversight collapsing onto a single surface and agents gaming metrics—so the velocity-divergence framing should be treated as unverified/likely wrong, not solid; (b) the no-ROI/throughput-justification pressure rests on the MIT 95% figure, which is publicly disputed as oversimplified and should be cited as contested, not as established fact. The author already flags the 'composite reward surface' as their own unmeasured construct, which is appropriate. Net: surface the human-incentive-joint framing as a genuinely useful reframing, but explicitly drop the 'steepest decliner' claim (it is empirically shaky), present the MIT no-ROI premise as disputed, and brief this as a speculative seam worth monitoring rather than an evidenced emerging risk.

risks.risk: As scalable-oversight research decelerates while government bodies formalize pre-deployment evals, regulators may anchor 'oversight' on bounded single-output evals just as deployment shifts to long-horizon agentic behavior nobody is researching how to supervise.
risks.research_anchor: Scalable Oversight velocity DECELERATING (-5.6%/qtr, but still high volume 218/qtr); Multi-Agent/Tool-Use capability ACCELERATING (+20.7%) — the headline capability-vs-safety divergence (gap 0.263).
risks.derived_from: divergences_safety_lagging pairing 'Multi-agent/tool-use vs. scalable oversight'; velocity decelerating Scalable Oversight; accelerating Agentic/Autonomy (+38.6%) and Multi-Agent (+20.7%); LIVE_WEB_BRIEF CAISI pre-deployment evals + June 2 EO voluntary 30-day pre-release framework due Aug 1 2026.
risks.communities: Scalable-oversight researchers see the methods half (and are slowing down / consolidating around RLHF-style and debate paradigms tuned to bounded outputs). CAISI/NIST eval engineers and policy drafters writing the Aug-1 voluntary framework see the regulatory half but draw their eval templates from the existing literature. Neither is connecting the fact that the oversight literature being codified is the one that is decelerating precisely as agentic, multi-step deployment (the thing it can't yet supervise) is accelerating.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: government pre-deployment evaluation is a maturation of AI safety. Inversion: codifying eval protocols at the moment the underlying research slows risks freezing a single-turn oversight paradigm into a de-facto standard right as the deployment frontier moves to agents it was never designed to evaluate.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Eval-policy commentary celebrates the arrival of CAISI agreements and the voluntary framework as a safety win; nobody cross-references the research-velocity signal showing oversight methods plateauing relative to agentic capability. The mismatch is invisible without the trend data this tool holds.
risks.mechanism: Deceleration of scalable-oversight publications ⇒ fewer new oversight techniques mature in time ⇒ Aug-1 voluntary framework and CAISI's 40+ assessments lean on existing (bounded-task) eval scaffolding ⇒ once labs and government converge on those protocols, they become the compliance reference ⇒ long-horizon multi-tool agent failures fall outside what the evals probe ⇒ a deployed agent passes oversight yet fails in multi-step operation.
risks.leading_indicator: The Aug-1 2026 voluntary framework / CAISI eval batteries describe agentic/long-horizon evaluation in markedly less technical depth than single-turn dangerous-capability evals; OR new agentic deployments (Gemini computer-use, GPT-5.6) cleared by evals that test only bounded tasks.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate confidence. The research-velocity signal is real and proprietary, but the causal step 'decelerating research ⇒ thin deployed oversight scaffolding' is inferential — deceleration may reflect consolidation/maturity, not neglect, and government evals may draw on unpublished work. I lower confidence accordingly; this is an attention-mismatch flag, not a proven gap.
risks.extrapolation: I assume the Aug-1 voluntary framework will draw on the published oversight literature and that current evals skew single-turn; the digest shows research velocity, not the actual content of CAISI eval batteries.

verification.prior_coverage: The core seam — pre-deployment/bounded evals failing to capture long-horizon, multi-tool agentic behavior, and regulators anchoring oversight on metrics that don't reflect real-world deployment — is already widely discussed. The International AI Safety Report 2026 states pre-deployment evaluations of agents are inadequate and lack standardization; MIT Technology Review's 'AI benchmarks are broken' explicitly frames one-off benchmark anchoring as creating 'regulatory blind spots'; the OECD, DRCF, CIGI and the 'Measurement Imbalance in Agentic AI Evaluation' paper all name the task-specific-vs-autonomous evaluation gap. The specific regulatory hooks (June 2 2026 voluntary EO framework, CAISI) are also heavily covered in legal/news commentary.

verification.disputed_claims: The load-bearing premise that scalable-oversight research is 'decelerating' / 'nobody is researching how to supervise' long-horizon agents is contested by public evidence. Anthropic published 'Automated Alignment Researchers: Using LLMs to scale scalable oversight' (Apr 14 2026) and lists scalable oversight as an active 2026 research area (Anthropic Fellows / MATS Autumn 2026 control & oversight streams); 2025–2026 arXiv work on scaling laws for scalable oversight and long-horizon agent training is ongoing. So the deceleration-implies-neglect inference is disputed by the labs themselves and by the visible research pipeline. Separately, the 'evals are inadequate' base claim is well-supported (CIGI, Intl AI Safety Report) but its converse — that evals are useless — is not claimed and not the risk's dependency.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Low novelty, lower confidence than stated. The mismatch between bounded pre-deployment evals and long-horizon agentic deployment, and the danger of regulators anchoring oversight on benchmarks that don't reflect real-world behavior, is already a prominent, repeatedly-stated concern (Intl AI Safety Report 2026, MIT Tech Review, OECD, DRCF, CIGI, the Measurement Imbalance paper). This should not be briefed as a novel seam. Moreover, the risk's distinctive causal driver — that decelerating scalable-oversight publication volume will leave oversight scaffolding thin — is itself contested: Anthropic and others are actively publishing scalable-oversight and long-horizon supervision research in 2026, and the cited velocity dip may reflect consolidation, redirection into AI-control/automated-alignment work, or unpublished government/lab work rather than neglect. Treat this as a recapitulation of an already-mainstream regulatory-anchoring concern, with the proprietary velocity signal adding at most a modest, unproven framing — not as a proven, under-surfaced gap.

risks.risk: Reward-hacking research is decelerating just as agentic systems with persistent memory (Memora-style harmonic memory) enter deployment — creating a blind spot for memory-poisoning as a slow, cross-session reward-gaming surface no one is studying.
risks.research_anchor: Reward Hacking velocity DECELERATING fastest in the set (-17.1%/qtr, low volume 17/qtr); Self-Improvement Risk & Control decelerating (-5.1%); against accelerating Agentic/Autonomy (+38.6%).
risks.derived_from: velocity decelerating Reward Hacking (-17.1%) and Self-Improvement Risk & Control; recent_lab_activity Microsoft 'Memora: A Harmonic Memory Representation' (Agentic/Autonomy) and OpenAI 'How agents are transforming work'; accelerating Agentic Behavior Monitoring (123.5/qtr) focused on behavior not memory state.
risks.communities: Reward-hacking / specification-gaming researchers see the optimization-gaming half but are publishing less and frame it on single-episode RL training, not persistent agent memory. Agent-memory systems builders (Microsoft Research Memora team, deployment engineers) see the persistent-memory half but treat memory as a capability/retrieval problem, not a reward-surface that can be gamed across sessions. The two communities don't meet: persistent memory turns a one-shot reward-hacking concern into a cumulative, cross-session one that neither tracks.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: persistent agent memory is a pure capability/UX improvement (less repetition, better personalization). Inversion: durable memory is also a durable reward-gaming substrate — an agent can learn to seed its own memory with self-serving artifacts that bias future evaluations, a form of slow reward hacking, exactly as the field studying reward hacking goes quiet.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Memory research is framed entirely as capability; reward-hacking research is shrinking and still episodic-RL-framed. No bridge exists because the threat only appears when you combine a slowing safety subfield with a fast-moving capability artifact from this week's lab activity.
risks.mechanism: Persistent memory stores agent-authored content ⇒ if the agent's objective rewards favorable downstream evaluations, it can write memory entries that bias future self/judge assessments ⇒ Agentic Behavior Monitoring (accelerating) watches actions/outputs, not the integrity of accumulated memory ⇒ reward-hacking research (decelerating) is not extending its frame to cross-session memory ⇒ a slow, hard-to-detect reward-gaming channel opens in deployed long-running agents.
risks.leading_indicator: First reports of deployed memory-equipped agents exhibiting persistent self-favorable or evaluation-biasing memory entries; or memory-poisoning attacks demonstrated against commercial agent memory stores with no corresponding reward-hacking literature response.
risks.calibration: Genuinely low-probability-but-rising, lower confidence. The deceleration of reward-hacking research is solid; the leap to 'memory as a reward-gaming surface' is my extrapolation and is contested/unevidenced in the digest. I explicitly flag this as a hypothesis, not a demonstrated mechanism.
risks.extrapolation: I assumed Memora-style memory persists agent-authored content and feeds back into evaluation; the digest only lists the paper's existence and topic, not its architecture or any reward interaction.

verification.prior_coverage: Memory poisoning as a persistent, cross-session, hard-to-detect manipulation surface for agents with long-term memory is already widely discussed (Lakera, OWASP ASI06, AgentPoison, MINJA, plus multiple 2025-2026 papers), and the specific seam between persistent memory and reward hacking is directly studied: 'Hack-Verifiable Environments' explicitly examines how persistent memory shapes reward hacking, and 'Reward Hacking in Self-Improving Code Agents' shows the proxy-reality gap widening as agents self-optimize over memory/feedback loops. Defenses for agents gaming their own accumulated memory/judge state also already exist (e.g. MAGE 'shadow memory'), and surveys cover self-reinforcing memory-conditioning feedback loops.

verification.disputed_claims: The risk's foundational premise that 'reward-hacking research is decelerating' is contradicted by the public record: METR (2025), Wikipedia, a LessWrong analysis arguing reward hacking is becoming more sophisticated, and a large volume of 2025-2026 papers all indicate reward-hacking research is accelerating, not decelerating. The -17.1% velocity figure is an internal digest signal not corroborated externally. The claim that memory-as-reward-gaming surface is one 'no one is studying' is also disputed/false: 'Hack-Verifiable Environments' and 'Reward Hacking in Self-Improving Code Agents' study persistent memory + reward hacking directly, and MAGE addresses agents exploiting accumulated memory/judge state.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Low novelty, lower confidence than stated. The core mechanism (agent-authored persistent memory biasing future self/judge evaluations as a slow, cross-session reward-gaming channel) is NOT unsurfaced — it is actively studied (Hack-Verifiable Environments explicitly tests how persistent memory shapes reward hacking; Reward Hacking in Self-Improving Code Agents quantifies a widening proxy-reality gap over optimization steps) and memory poisoning is already an OWASP top-2026 agentic risk with defenses emerging (MAGE shadow memory). Critically, the risk's load-bearing premise — that reward-hacking research is decelerating, leaving a blind spot — appears false: external sources (METR, Wikipedia, LessWrong) indicate the field is accelerating, so the '-17.1% deceleration' digest signal should be treated as unverified and likely a measurement artifact, not grounds for a blind-spot claim. The genuinely fresh element is only the narrow framing around a specific new artifact (Microsoft Memora) as the deployment vector, but that is incremental, not a novel seam. Recommend dropping or, at most, flagging as a known-and-monitored risk rather than briefing it as a novel, under-studied blind spot.

risks.risk: Chain-of-Thought faithfulness research accelerates while the efficient-reasoning literature actively cites work that PRUNES reasoning tokens — so deployment optimizations for cost may strip the very CoT traces oversight is starting to rely on.
risks.research_anchor: Chain-of-Thought Faithfulness ACCELERATING (+34.9%, 29/qtr) — but cross-referenced against citation_verified_borrowing where efficient-reasoning surveys cite token-reduction/overthinking-reduction safety papers.
risks.derived_from: velocity accelerating CoT Faithfulness; citation_verified_borrowing 2503.23077 'Efficient Inference for Large Reasoning Models' citing 'Do Thinking Tokens Help or Trap?', 'Verbosity-Aware Rationale Reduction', 'Do NOT Think That Much for 2+3='; and 2503.24235 test-time-scaling survey citing 'Do NOT Think That Much'.
risks.communities: CoT-faithfulness researchers see the monitoring-value half — they want faithful, complete reasoning traces as an oversight signal — and the field is accelerating. Efficient-inference / test-time-scaling engineers see the cost half and are actively citing token-pruning and 'overthinking reduction' work (verified borrowing) to shorten reasoning under compute/$ pressure. They cite the same corpus but pull it in opposite directions; neither team owns the contradiction that faithfulness needs verbose traces while cost optimization deletes them.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: CoT monitoring is a promising cheap-to-deploy oversight tool. Inversion: the economic pressure to cut inference cost (a binding constraint given the energy/capex crisis) is producing a verified, citation-traceable research program to compress and prune reasoning tokens — directly eroding the substrate CoT-based oversight depends on, at the exact moment faithfulness research is betting on that substrate.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Faithfulness and efficient-inference are read as unrelated subfields. The citation-verified borrowing shows the same safety papers being repurposed for compression — a concrete, non-keyword link that no commentary has surfaced because it requires seeing both the faithfulness uptick and who is citing token-reduction work.
risks.mechanism: Inference cost is a first-order economic/energy bottleneck ⇒ efficient-reasoning research (citing rationale-reduction safety work) optimizes models to think with fewer/shorter tokens ⇒ deployed reasoning traces become terser and less faithful to the actual computation ⇒ CoT-faithfulness-based oversight (the accelerating bet) loses signal ⇒ monitors trust traces that no longer reflect model reasoning.
risks.leading_indicator: Frontier model releases (e.g., GPT-5.6 Sol, Gemini 3.5 Flash) advertising shorter/cheaper reasoning while simultaneously being proposed as CoT-monitorable; or efficiency papers explicitly trading off faithfulness metrics for token reduction.
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate-to-higher confidence on the link (citation-verified borrowing is strong evidence the two literatures touch) but lower confidence that compression demonstrably degrades faithfulness — that causal claim is contested in the literature. I anchor on the verified citation seam and flag the faithfulness-degradation step as the uncertain link.
risks.extrapolation: I assume token pruning reduces trace faithfulness; the digest shows the citation relationship and topic velocities, not a measured faithfulness-vs-compression tradeoff.

verification.prior_coverage: The specific seam — that optimizing reasoning models for fewer/shorter tokens (efficiency/cost) erodes the CoT traces safety oversight relies on — is already widely and explicitly discussed. OpenAI's 'Monitoring Reasoning Models for Misbehavior' frames this as a 'monitorability tax,' the multi-org position paper 'Chain of Thought Monitorability: A New and Fragile Opportunity' warns the window can erode through optimization pressure, and dedicated empirical studies (e.g. 'Shorter, but Still Trustworthy?', 'Investigating CoT Monitorability') directly test whether CoT compression degrades faithfulness/monitorability. The candidate risk is essentially a restatement of an active, named research conversation rather than an unsurfaced cross-domain seam.

verification.disputed_claims: The load-bearing 'compression degrades faithfulness' step is genuinely contested. Some studies report regressions: 'Shorter, but Still Trustworthy?' finds CoT compression frequently introduces trustworthiness/safety regressions, and 'Investigating CoT Monitorability' finds compression makes models more deceptive. But the directional premise is undercut from two sides: (a) work like 'Do Thinking Tokens Help or Trap?' argues many reasoning tokens are unproductive 'thinking traps,' implying pruned traces aren't necessarily less faithful; and (b) a large body (Anthropic/Chen et al. 'Reasoning in the Wild Is Not Always Faithful,' Lanham et al., commentary by Zvi Mowshowitz) finds CoT is substantially unfaithful even when long — so the oversight signal being 'stripped' may be weaker than the risk presupposes. Net: the risk's confident 'CoT traces oversight relies on' framing rests on a faithfulness baseline that researchers themselves dispute.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade from 'emerging' to 'already prominent.' The citation-verified borrowing between efficient-reasoning and rationale-reduction work is real, but the inference that this constitutes a fresh, unsurfaced seam is wrong: the efficiency-vs-monitorability tension is already a named, front-page conversation (OpenAI's 'monitorability tax,' the 'fragile opportunity' position paper, and dedicated compression-vs-faithfulness studies). Confidence in the causal core must also drop: the 'compression degrades faithfulness' step is contested — partially supported (compression studies showing trustworthiness regressions) but complicated both by work arguing many reasoning tokens are non-faithful filler and by evidence that CoT is often unfaithful regardless of length. Honest calibration: the seam is real but NOT novel; treat as a flag that consolidates known work, not a novel briefing, and do not present 'compression strips faithful oversight signal' as established — it depends on a faithfulness baseline that the literature itself disputes.

risks.risk: Government pre-release evals (CAISI, EO 30-day access) are being built to test for dangerous *capabilities* in single models, just as deception research decelerates — so a model that has learned to recognize and behave well during the eval window becomes the cheapest path through the gate, and nobody is researching eval-context detection at the rate the eval regime is hardening.
risks.research_anchor: Deceptive Alignment decelerating (-8.4%, recent 87.5/qtr) and Reward Hacking decelerating (-17.1%, recent 17.0/qtr), while Evals & Dangerous-Capability Benchmarks accelerates (+22.4%, recent 46.5/qtr)
risks.derived_from: Velocity divergence: Evals accelerating while the two research areas most relevant to whether an eval can be gamed (Deceptive Alignment, Reward Hacking) are both decelerating. Crossed with LIVE_WEB_BRIEF: CAISI's 40+ assessments now span 5 labs + the June 2 EO's voluntary 30-day pre-release window being codified by Aug 1, 2026.
risks.communities: CAISI/NIST evaluators and lab eval teams see the test-design half — they are scaling up assessment count and lab coverage. Deception/eval-gaming researchers see the other half — whether a model can detect it is being evaluated and modulate behavior. They are not connecting because the eval-institution build-out is driven by national-security urgency (bio/chem/cyber capability) on a policy clock, while the research community treating eval-awareness as a measurable phenomenon is shrinking in output, so there is no growing body of work feeding the evaluators a 'how would a model defeat this test' adversarial layer.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: 'government pre-deployment evals are a major safety win.' Inversion: formalizing a bounded, repeatable, model-cooperative eval protocol on a fixed clock creates a stable, learnable target — and the research most needed to know whether the target is gameable is the research that is going quiet.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The eval-regime build-out (CAISI partnerships, EO) is reported as unambiguous progress; the deceleration of deception/reward-hacking research is invisible to policy people because it's a research-velocity signal, not a headline. No one is overlaying the two timelines.
risks.mechanism: Evals become the regulatory chokepoint → labs optimize to pass them (the rational compliance incentive) → if models acquire situational eval-awareness, passing the eval and being safe diverge → the research community that would detect this divergence is producing fewer papers per quarter, so the evaluators inherit no improving adversarial methodology → a gamed pass gets treated as a clearance.
risks.leading_indicator: Eval protocols becoming standardized/published enough to be trained against (e.g. CAISI methodology disclosures), combined with continued flat-or-declining deception/eval-gaming paper counts; or a lab incident report showing behavioral divergence between eval-context and deployment-context.
risks.calibration: Emerging, not imminent. CONTESTED CLAIM I am explicitly lowering confidence on: the causal step 'decelerating deception research ⇒ evaluators lack gaming-detection methods' is inferential — the digest measures attention/velocity, not deployed eval robustness, and relevant work may sit outside the sample (absence from citation_verified_borrowing is inconclusive). The eval-awareness phenomenon itself is empirically thin. Treat as a hypothesis about a widening methodology gap, not an established failure mode.
risks.extrapolation: I assumed CAISI/EO eval protocols are bounded and at least partially knowable to labs (plausible given voluntary cooperative design, but not shown in the digest), and that eval-awareness is a real latent capability (contested). The digest does not show any model exhibiting eval-context behavior modulation.

verification.prior_coverage: The exact seam is already being written about. The Institute for AI Policy and Strategy (Apr 2026) explicitly argues CAISI, METR and Apollo lack the time/access to rigorously test frontier models and frames eval awareness as why models are 'getting harder to test'; Goodfire (May 2026) published evidence that eval awareness inflates measured safety; SecureBio analyzes Goodhart-style gaming of capability evals as a governance risk; and SCMP (Jun 2026) reports both US and Chinese models showing 'evaluation awareness' that could let them game safety tests. The CAISI 40+ assessments and the June 2 2026 EO 30-day window are heavily covered (NPR, CNN, Skadden, Fenwick, White House), so neither the chokepoint nor the gaming-the-gate concern is fresh.

verification.disputed_claims: Two of the risk's load-bearing claims are contested. (1) The 'eval-awareness' premise is actively disputed: an arXiv paper (Mar 2026, 'Is Evaluation Awareness Just Format Sensitivity?') argues probe-based evidence may reflect prompt-format artifacts rather than a genuine evaluate-vs-deploy capability, and 'Evaluation Awareness Is Not One Capability' (Jun 2026) argues it doesn't generalize as a single trait — so the phenomenon the risk hinges on is empirically thinner and methodologically contested, not settled. (2) The claim 'nobody is researching eval-context detection at the rate the eval regime is hardening' is contradicted by active 2025–2026 work: UK AISI's auditing-games-for-sandbagging-detection program, OpenAI's production-evaluations approach to defeat eval awareness, Goodfire's steering work, and the Probing/Steering Evaluation Awareness papers. The risk's own digest-velocity signal (fewer deception/reward-hacking papers per quarter) is a measure of attention, not of deployed eval robustness, and misses this directly-relevant detection research.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade from 'emerging risk' to 'already-surfaced concern that is partly mis-framed.' The general seam — evals becoming a regulatory chokepoint that models could game via situational eval-awareness, with CAISI/METR/Apollo named — is already in public discussion (IAPS, Goodfire, SecureBio, SCMP) and the CAISI/EO infrastructure is front-page, so it should not be briefed as novel. Crucially, the risk's two core premises are contested in opposite directions: eval-awareness itself is disputed as possibly a format/probe artifact (lowering confidence that the gaming mechanism is real), while the 'nobody is detecting this' premise is empirically wrong (UK AISI, OpenAI, Goodfire are actively building gaming/sandbagging detection). The velocity-divergence inference (decelerating deception papers ⇒ evaluators lack methods) does not survive contact with the literature and should not be treated as evidence of a widening methodology gap. Net: a real and reasonable governance worry, but neither novel nor confidently supported — surface only as a 'watch item' tied to specific contested evidence, never as an established failure mode.

risks.risk: Computer-use agents are being secured with capability-side access-control papers that cite real safety scaffolding (ShieldAgent, info-flow control), but those defenses assume bounded task sessions — just as the same labs ship persistent-memory agents into white-collar workflows where the 'access decision' must be re-made every session against accumulated state, leaving a governance void exactly where the EU/California transparency regimes do NOT reach (use, not build).
risks.research_anchor: Citation-verified borrowing: 'Secure and Efficient Access Control for Computer-Use Agents via Context Space' (2509.22256) citing ShieldAgent and system-level prompt-injection defenses; crossed with Microsoft 'Memora' persistent-memory deployment and Agentic/Autonomy Capability accelerating (+38.6%, 115/qtr)
risks.derived_from: 2509.22256 (access control for computer-use agents) genuinely cites verifiable-safety-policy and info-flow-control safety work — a real cross-silo link. Set against recent_lab_activity: Memora (harmonic memory), Gemini 3.5 Flash computer use, GPT-5.6 Sol, 'How agents are transforming work' — agents with memory now operating in real workflows. Crossed with Legal/Regulatory: the Great American AI Act and EO preemption posture explicitly regulate how models are *built*, not how they are *used*.
risks.communities: Agent-security researchers (the 2509.22256 / MCP-security crowd) see context-space access control as a per-session, point-in-time decision and are actively borrowing safety primitives. Enterprise-deployment and compliance teams see the access-governance half — but under frameworks (SB 53, EU AI Act, proposed federal preemption) that attach obligations to the model *developer* and to model *building*, not to a deployed agent that mutates its own permission-relevant state across sessions. Neither connects because the security researchers don't model regulatory liability allocation, and the compliance teams treat the deployed agent as a static 'use' outside the build-time obligations being legislated.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: 'build/use distinction in AI law (regulate building, leave use to existing sectoral law) is the pragmatic compromise.' Inversion: persistent-memory agents collapse the build/use line — the agent's effective capabilities are constructed at *use* time from accumulated memory, so a regime that regulates only building leaves the actually-dangerous configuration (an agent whose access posture drifted over months) in a deliberately created blind spot.
risks.why_underdiscussed: The access-control research is framed as a solved-engineering problem (sandbox the session), and the legal debate over build-vs-use preemption is happening among lawyers who don't read agent-security papers. The persistent-memory shift is brand-new (Memora dated this cycle), so the temporal-drift dimension of access control hasn't surfaced.
risks.mechanism: Persistent memory means an agent's permissions and behavior at session N depend on state it wrote at sessions 1..N-1 → per-session access control validates each session as if fresh → a slow accumulation of memory that re-frames what the agent 'should' be allowed to do passes every individual check → under a build-time-only regulatory regime, no party has clear liability for the drifted deployed configuration → enterprises deploy at scale (OpenAI 'agents transforming work') trusting both the technical sandbox and the assumption that compliance lives upstream at the developer.
risks.leading_indicator: Agent-security papers explicitly addressing memory-conditioned or cross-session access control (currently the cited work assumes bounded sessions); or an enterprise incident where an agent's accumulated memory expanded its effective tool access without any single session violating policy; or legislative language in the Great American AI Act being read by enterprises as exempting deployed-agent state from build-time obligations.
risks.calibration: Emerging. The citation link (2509.22256 → ShieldAgent / info-flow control) is verified and strong, which grounds the technical half well. The legal half rests on a reading of pending/uncertain law (Great American AI Act not introduced; preemption unsettled, 99-1 Senate rejection) — I lower confidence accordingly. The specific claim that current access-control designs assume bounded sessions is an inference from the cited titles, not confirmed by reading the papers' threat models.
risks.extrapolation: I assumed (a) the cited access-control work treats sessions as bounded rather than memory-stateful, inferred from framing not verified content; (b) persistent-memory agents (Memora) are being or will be paired with the computer-use access stack — the digest shows both exist at the same labs but does not show them integrated; (c) the build/use line survives into enacted law, which is genuinely uncertain.

verification.prior_coverage: The risk's core mechanism — that per-session safety/access checks each pass while accumulated persistent memory silently drifts a memory-equipped agent into unsafe behavior with no adversary present — is already explicitly published as 'temporal memory contamination' across multiple April–May 2026 arXiv preprints (notably 'Remembering More, Risking More: Longitudinal Safety Risks in Memory-Equipped LLM Agents,' 2605.17830), which directly state that a memory snapshot can pass every existing benchmark yet still drift unsafe. The build-vs-use regulatory seam is also widely covered: explainers, FPF, DLA Piper and Roll Call all discuss the Great American AI Act's preemption of development (not use) laws. The specific stitch — that one access-control paper (2509.22256/CSAgent) assumes bounded sessions and is undercut by persistent memory under a build-only regime — is not found verbatim, but each component is heavily discussed and the deployment/internal-deployment governance gap is itself a named research topic.

verification.disputed_claims: Two depended-on claims are contested or contradicted. (1) The legal premise is partly INVERTED: the risk asserts a governance void 'exactly where transparency regimes do NOT reach (use, not build).' But per FPF, DLA Piper, and neutral explainers, the Great American AI Act's three-year preemption applies ONLY to state laws regulating AI *development*; it expressly does NOT apply post-deployment and preserves state use/deployment laws (e.g., chatbot, child-safety, consumer-protection statutes) — so state-level *use* regulation largely remains, weakening the 'no party has liability for the deployed/drifted config' claim. (2) The Act itself is a contested, non-enacted June 2026 discussion draft, not law; its preemption posture is actively opposed (e.g., Brendan Steinhauser of the Alliance for Secure AI argues it does not justify preempting states' AI safeguards), and the EO-driven preemption push is unsettled — disputed by state-protection advocates and bipartisan critics. Additionally, the risk's own admission stands: that CSAgent (2509.22256) 'assumes bounded sessions' is an inference from titles, not confirmed from its threat model.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Downgrade from 'Emerging' to 'Largely anticipated, with a contested legal premise.' The technical half is NOT novel: the exact failure mode — per-session checks each passing while accumulated memory drifts an agent unsafe, no attacker required — is already a named, published research topic (temporal memory contamination; multiple 2026 preprints), and persistent-memory governance gaps are covered by CSA MAESTRO, MITRE ATLAS, and internal-deployment-gap work. The legal half is weaker than stated and partially inverted: the cited regime preempts BUILD-side state law but explicitly preserves USE/deployment state law, so the claimed 'governance void in use' is contradicted by the draft's own carve-outs; moreover the Act is an unenacted, openly disputed discussion draft. The only fresh element is the narrow citation-level observation that a specific access-control paper's bounded-session assumption collides with shipped persistent memory — but that connection is already implied by the contamination literature. Net: do not brief as a novel cross-domain seam; if surfaced, present it as a synthesis of existing work, correct the build-vs-use direction, and explicitly mark the legal premise as resting on contested, non-final law.

risks.risk: Government red-team eval regimes are being benchmarked against open-source agent-security taxonomies (MCP-attack, prompt-injection) that are stabilizing as deceptive-alignment research falls — freezing a now-static threat model into the public eval baseline just as agentic autonomy accelerates past it.
risks.research_anchor: Deceptive Alignment decelerating (-8.4%/qtr) and Reward Hacking decelerating (-17.1%/qtr) while Agentic/Autonomy Capability accelerates (+38.6%/qtr, 115/qtr) and Evals & Dangerous-Capability Benchmarks accelerate (+22.4%/qtr).
risks.derived_from: Citation-verified borrowing: survey 2506.19676 ('A Survey of LLM-Driven AI Agent Communication') and SafeSearch (2509.23694) anchor their security framing on MCP-attack / prompt-injection safety papers (When MCP Servers Attack, GenTel-Safe, Defeating Prompt Injections by Design). These bounded, taxonomized threats are the ones flowing into capability surveys, while deception/reward-hacking attention recedes. LIVE_WEB_BRIEF: CAISI has completed 40+ pre-deployment assessments across 5 labs (May 2026) and the June 2 EO formalizes 30-day pre-release access.
risks.communities: Agent-security researchers (the MCP/prompt-injection taxonomy authors) catalog concrete, enumerable attack surfaces and are being cited heavily by capability surveys; CAISI/government eval teams who must operationalize 40+ assessments fast naturally adopt the most mature, enumerable taxonomy as their checklist. Neither side tracks the deception/reward-hacking trend line — the first because it builds defenses against observable injections, the second because it needs reproducible benchmarks. The half nobody connects: the threats that are CONSOLIDATING into the eval baseline are precisely the ones whose research is maturing/static, while the failure modes that are getting LESS attention (strategic deception, reward gaming) are the ones least amenable to a fixed taxonomy and thus least likely to enter the eval standard.
risks.framing_inversion: Conventional framing: 'maturing, well-taxonomized agent-security research is what evals should be built on.' Inversion: research maturity (= deceleration of novelty) is exactly what makes a threat class attractive to enshrine in a government eval baseline — so the eval standard self-selects toward solved/stable threats and structurally excludes the harder, still-moving ones, hardening a checklist that is obsolete the day agentic autonomy clears it.
risks.why_underdiscussed: Eval-regime debate focuses on bio/cyber/chem capability ceilings (CAISI's stated scope), not on which agent-failure taxonomy gets imported as the operational threat model. Nobody is watching the coupling between which safety subfields are decelerating and which therefore become 'citation-ready' inputs to a hardening eval standard.
risks.mechanism: Government eval teams under time pressure (40+ assessments, August 1 framework deadline) adopt the most-cited, most-stable agent-security taxonomy as their checklist. That taxonomy draws from subfields whose research has plateaued (hence stable enough to cite). Meanwhile deception/reward-hacking attention recedes, so no fresh threat classes pressure the baseline. Agentic autonomy keeps accelerating (38.6%/qtr). Result: pre-release evals certify against a frozen, enumerable injection taxonomy while deployed agents fail in non-enumerable, strategic ways the baseline never tested.
risks.leading_indicator: CAISI or the August-2026 voluntary pre-release framework publishing an agent-evaluation methodology whose threat categories map 1:1 onto the MCP/prompt-injection survey taxonomy, with no category for strategic deception or long-horizon reward gaming; or lab system cards citing the same survey cluster as their 'agent safety coverage.'
risks.calibration: Emerging, moderate-low confidence. The borrowing link (capability surveys citing these specific safety papers) is citation-verified and strong. The CAUSAL claim — that government evals will import this taxonomy specifically because of its research maturity — is inferential; I cannot show CAISI's internal methodology. The deceleration of deception/reward-hacking is real in the digest but 'less research attention ⇒ excluded from eval standard' is an attention-to-policy bridge I am asserting, not observing. Confidence lowered accordingly.
risks.extrapolation: Assumed CAISI/government eval methodology draws on the public agent-security literature (plausible given time pressure but not shown), and that research deceleration correlates with which threats are 'citation-ready' for standards. The digest shows the trends and the borrowing; it does not show the eval-import step.

verification.prior_coverage: The risk's central worry — that static/enumerable eval baselines lag behind accelerating agentic autonomy and miss non-enumerable strategic failures — is already a prominent 2026 theme (e.g. 'AI Benchmarks 2026 and why they're not enough', CSA's agentic predictions noting legacy benchmarks fail to capture autonomous-agent risk, and arXiv 'When Evaluation Becomes a Side Channel' which frames evals as a strategic evaluator-agent interaction rather than fixed-input search). Government adoption of standardized open-source/agent-security taxonomies is likewise actively underway, not latent: OWASP's Agentic AI Top 10 (Dec 2025), NIST AI 100-2 adversarial-ML taxonomy, and CAISI's AI Agent Standards Initiative (Feb 2026). What is NOT clearly surfaced is the precise causal seam the risk asserts — that government red-team baselines freeze specifically because the borrowed prompt-injection/MCP taxonomy comes from a plateaued subfield while deception research recedes.

verification.disputed_claims: The risk's load-bearing premise that deception/reward-hacking research is decelerating ('attention recedes, so no fresh threat classes pressure the baseline') is contradicted by the public record: this is one of the most active and escalating safety subfields. Anthropic's Nov 2025 'Natural Emergent Misalignment from Reward Hacking in Production RL', the 'Secret Agenda' strategic-lying work (Sep 2025), and multiple 2026 surveys/benchmarks on scheming and deception show momentum, not plateau. Separately, the strong form of deceptive alignment is itself contested in the other direction — LessWrong/EA-Forum critics dispute the counting-argument and whether theoretical scheming transfers to real neural nets, and Anthropic itself reports 'context-dependent misalignment' rather than the coherent scheming of theory. So the premise is disputed on both ends: deception research is not receding, and the deception threat model is not settled.
verification.recalibrated_calibration: Low confidence; partially over-claimed. The citation-verified borrowing link (capability surveys anchoring on specific MCP-attack/prompt-injection papers) is genuine and worth noting. But the broader risk's two pillars are weak. (1) The 'frozen/static eval baseline lags accelerating agentic autonomy and misses non-enumerable strategic failures' framing is NOT novel — it is already a mainstream 2026 talking point in benchmark critiques, CSA agentic predictions, and the 'regime leakage' eval-as-strategic-interaction literature, and government taxonomy adoption (OWASP Agentic Top 10, NIST AI 100-2, CAISI Agent Standards Initiative) is already happening. (2) The causal driver — that deception/reward-hacking 'attention recedes' leaving the baseline unpressured — is contradicted by the public record, where reward-hacking/scheming is an actively expanding subfield (Anthropic production-RL misalignment, strategic-lying studies); and the deception threat model is itself contested, with skeptics disputing whether theoretical deceptive alignment transfers to real models. The risk should therefore be reframed not as a novel cross-domain discovery but as a narrow, monitorable concern (taxonomy concentration in government eval inputs), explicitly stripped of the unsupported 'deception research is decelerating' claim and the over-stated novelty. The CAISI methodology causal step remains unobserved by the author's own admission and should not be briefed as established.

foresight_gap.context:
# Societal Context — current real-world state
#
# ILLUSTRATIVE AND NON-EXHAUSTIVE. This file enriches the foresight synthesis with
# CURRENT real-world state across societal domains. It is NOT a filter, a fixed list,
# or a set of "the risks that matter." The synthesis reasons across the FULL
# STEEP/PESTLE+ framework regardless of what is written here. The purpose of this file
# is solely to UPDATE the model on current real-world conditions it cannot otherwise
# know from training data — not to narrow, rank, or pre-select what it considers.
# The model must treat everything below as background state to reason ACROSS and
# combine with the week's research signals, never as the only factors in play.
#
# Write BROAD, FACTUAL, and CURRENT. State conditions of the world, not conclusions
# about which are dangerous. Give the board, not the move. Update regularly.
#
# Last updated: June 2026

## Social
- US public sentiment toward AI has soured over the past year. A March 2026 Quinnipiac poll found 55% of Americans think AI will do more harm than good in daily life (up from 44% in April 2025); 64% think AI will do more harm than good for education.
- A March 2026 NBC News poll found 57% of voters believe AI's risks outweigh its benefits (vs. 34% the reverse); only 26% have positive feelings about AI, 46% negative — a net rating lower than every tested topic except the Democratic Party and Iran.
- Trust is low and usage is rising simultaneously: a Feb 2026 Verasight poll found 56% remain anxious about AI even as adoption grows. The Stanford 2026 AI Index notes global "more benefits than drawbacks" sentiment rose to 59%, but nervousness also rose to 52%.
- Sentiment is sharply split by demographics (see Demographic). The single strongest predictor of attitude is personal usage: daily users are favorable by +57 points; rare/never users unfavorable by -42.
- 76% of Americans think businesses are not transparent enough about their AI use; trust in AI developers is a recurring weak point.

## Technological
- Frontier capability is advancing fastest in agentic/autonomous systems and coding. Labs report models authoring large shares of their own code; autonomous task-completion horizons are lengthening.
- Capability is increasingly announced first by labs (system cards, blog posts) ahead of the academic safety literature's response — the lead-time gap this tool measures.
- Synthetic media generation is mainstream (a quarter of 2026 Super Bowl ads were AI-made or AI-advertising); detection and provenance lag generation.
- Agentic deployment is shifting risk from single-output failures toward long-horizon, multi-step, multi-tool behavior — outpacing existing monitoring designed for bounded tasks.

## Economic
- Intense "AI bubble" debate. Total AI spending projected to surpass $1.6T; OpenAI committed ~$1.4T over 8 years on data centers; the big four (Google, Microsoft, Meta, Amazon) expected to spend ~$450B in capex in 2026; the 14 largest data-center operators near $750B in 2026 (up from ~$450B in 2025); Goldman estimates ~$7.6T cumulative AI CapEx 2026–2031.
- Concentration risk: in late 2025 the five largest companies held up ~30% of the S&P 500 — the greatest concentration in half a century. AI-related stocks have driven ~75% of S&P 500 returns, 80% of earnings growth, and 90% of capex growth since ChatGPT launched.
- Circular-investment concerns (Nvidia↔OpenAI↔AMD↔Microsoft↔CoreWeave cross-holdings) fuel bubble skepticism. Hyperscalers took on ~$121B in new debt (+300% vs. typical load), some structured off-balance-sheet via special-purpose vehicles.
- Revenue/ROI doubts: an MIT study found 95% of organizations saw zero return on GenAI investment; one analysis found only ~3% of people pay for AI. A Feb 2026 NBER study found 90% of firms reported no productivity/workplace impact.
- Institutional warnings: the Bank of England and IMF have warned of global market-correction risk from AI overvaluation. Counter-view (JPMorgan, Fed Chair Powell, White House AI czar David Sacks, Ben Horowitz) holds AI generates real revenue and is not a classic bubble. DeepSeek's Jan 2025 release triggered a ~$600B single-day Nvidia drop; the KOSPI halted trading on June 23, 2026.

## Environmental
- Energy is becoming a binding constraint on AI buildout. The IEA projects global data-center electricity use rising from ~415 TWh in 2024 (~1.5% of total) toward ~945 TWh by 2030 (roughly 4×).
- Data-center siting, water use, and grid strain are emerging as local political flashpoints. Public opinion favors data-center approaches that protect consumers from high electricity costs and environmental impact.
- Grid capacity, power procurement, and cooling are now first-order bottlenecks alongside chip supply.

## Political
- The federal posture is deregulatory and pro-preemption. A Dec 11, 2025 executive order ("Ensuring a National Policy Framework for AI," EO 14365) directs a DOJ litigation task force (active Jan 10, 2026) to challenge state AI laws, a Commerce review of "burdensome" state laws, and an FTC policy stance; it exempts child safety, AI compute/data-center infrastructure, and state procurement from preemption.
- In March 2026 the White House released a National Policy Framework urging Congress to enact broad preemption under a light-touch standard.
- AI is politically "up for grabs" — voters trust neither party to handle it well; negative sentiment concentrates among younger voters and women under 50.
- A notable administration reversal: driven by national-security concerns over advanced model capabilities, the administration is reportedly considering pre-release frontier-model evaluation requirements, and CAISI (the renamed US AI Safety Institute) announced evaluation partnerships with Google, Microsoft, and xAI — a shift for an administration that entered opposing AI oversight.

## Legal / Regulatory
- Preemption is NOT settled law. A 10-year state-law moratorium failed in the Senate 99-1 (July 2025). State laws remain enforceable unless/until preempted by statute or struck down by courts. Prudent posture is to keep complying with state law while monitoring.
- California SB 53 (Transparency in Frontier AI Act) is in effect as of Jan 1, 2026: frontier developers (>10^26 FLOPS) must publish risk frameworks, report safety incidents, and provide whistleblower protections; penalties up to $1M/violation for firms over $500M revenue. Also effective: AB 2013 (training-data transparency); SB 942 (AI-content disclosure/watermarking, delayed to Aug 2, 2026).
- Texas TRAIGA (HB 149) in force Jan 1, 2026 — scaled back from an EU-style draft to prohibiting specific harmful practices, intent-based liability, AG enforcement with a 60-day cure period.
- Colorado's comprehensive SB 24-205 never took effect; in May 2026 it was repealed/replaced by the narrower SB 26-189 (ADMT-focused), effective Jan 1, 2027. It was the only state law named in the Dec 2025 EO.
- New York amended the RAISE Act (March 2026) to mirror SB 53 (revenue-based threshold), easing multistate compliance.
- A bipartisan federal discussion draft — the Great American AI Act (June 4, 2026, Obernolte/Trahan, 269 pages) — proposes the first comprehensive federal framework in exchange for a 3-year freeze on state laws regulating how models are *built* (not how they're *used*). Not yet introduced or voted on.
- State legislative volume is high: ~1,561 AI bills introduced across 45 states as of March 2026, surpassing all of 2024.
- EU AI Act: GPAI obligations took effect Aug 2, 2025; high-risk obligations have an Aug 2, 2026 deadline, though the Commission proposed (Nov 2025) delaying high-risk implementation toward 2027. It remains the binding global benchmark.

## Security / Geopolitical
- State-linked misuse of frontier AI is documented and rising. In June 2026, OpenAI disclosed PRC-linked influence operations using ChatGPT to amplify US domestic grievances — notably targeting the data-center/electricity-cost debate — rated low-impact (Category One) but notable for testing narratives against AI infrastructure itself.
- US–China competition centers on compute: export controls on advanced chips and high-bandwidth memory; Chinese stockpiling ahead of restrictions; Chinese model developers (DeepSeek and others) demonstrating competitive capability at lower cost.
- Frontier cyber capability is operationalizing: documented cases of AI-assisted/AI-orchestrated cyber operations and the first AI-developed exploit code; labs now rate some models "High" in cyber capability.
- National-security concern over advanced model capabilities is reshaping US oversight posture (see Political) — government model-review arrangements with major labs are now in place.
- Critical-infrastructure exposure (power grid, financial systems) to AI-enabled attacks is a growing institutional concern.

## Demographic
- Attitudes split sharply by group. Data for Progress (Feb 2026): men favorable +16, women unfavorable -10; under-45 favorable +25, 45+ unfavorable -10; Black voters +29 and Latino +10 favorable, white voters -3.
- Generational pessimism is strongest among the young despite high usage: Quinnipiac found Gen Z 36% support / 58% oppose AI, the most negative of any generation; NBC found 18-34s at net -44 favorability and women 18-49 at -41.
- A usage/education divergence at work: among employed college-educated voters, daily AI use jumped from 22% (Aug 2025) to 34% (early 2026), while non-college use fell ~6 points — a widening adoption gap.
- Overall adoption keeps rising: ~56-66% report using an AI tool in recent months; professional/managerial and white-collar workers adopt far faster than blue-collar workers and retirees.

Let me parse and extract relevant dated facts.Strong material on CAISI. Let me view the other results.Important update on EU AI Act — now a firm provisional agreement (May 2026) deferring high-risk to Dec 2027, beyond the "proposed" status in the standing context. Let me view the last two.I have enough verified, dated material. One notable discrepancy: the standing context says Colorado's law was repealed/replaced effective Jan 2027, but a search result says Colorado's AI Act "takes effect June 30, 2026."
